title: Request Construction, Method Semantics, Safety, and Idempotency · Browser and Node Client Systems · Guided Contract Studio
summary: An accessibility product team integrating a staging browser API must build a first protocol model from narrated fixtures and explain each policy boundary for request construction, method semantics, safety, and idempotency, producing method-aware request builder. Planned artifact: Build a complete first version of a TypeScript 7 standards-based Fetch method-aware request builder while every earlier competency remains active. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case pra…
objectives: Construct a request with an intentional method, target, fields, body, context, and caller-owned policy. Demonstrate the result through TypeScript 7 strict types plus WHATWG Fetch Request, Response, Headers, URL, streams, and AbortSignal contracts.

Distinguish safe and idempotent method semantics from what one particular server happens to do. Demonstrate the result through TypeScript 7 strict types plus WHATWG Fetch Request, Response, Headers, URL, streams, and AbortSignal contracts.

Use GET and HEAD with conditional, range, cache, and body expectations that preserve their defined semantics. Demonstrate the result through TypeScript 7 strict types plus WHATWG Fetch Request, Response, Headers, URL, streams, and AbortSignal contracts.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: Fetch and TypeScript Commit to the mission model · Workshop Request Components
steps.instruction:
workshop case — accessible staging dashboard SDK must repeat request T-3 after an uncertain timeout: GET status, POST job creation, PUT replacement, and PATCH transition are candidates. Classify safety, idempotency, body replay, and If-Match evidence before choosing. Competency probe: Construct a request with an intentional method, target, fields, body, context, and caller-owned policy. Demonstrate the result through TypeScript 7 strict types plus WHATWG Fetch Request, Response, Headers, URL, streams, and AbortSignal contracts.

What must be established before implementation?

Evidence target: Fetch and TypeScript Commit to the mission model · Workshop Request Components.
steps.why: The decision supplies observable evidence for construct a request with an intentional method, target, fields, body, context, and caller-owned policy. demonstrate the result through typescript 7 strict types plus whatwg fetch request, response, headers, url, streams, and abortsignal contracts. For Fetch and TypeScript Commit to the mission model · Workshop Request Components, keep Request Components connected to executable HTTP client decision evidence.

At Fetch and TypeScript Test an acceptance boundary · Workshop Preconditions Concurrency, trace this concrete input and predict resolution, redirect, status, representation, retry, and cleanup evidence before running it: accessible staging dashboard SDK must repeat request T-6 after an uncertain timeout: GET status, POST job creation, PUT replacement, and PATCH transition are candidates. Classify safety, idempotency, body replay, and If-Match evidence before choosing. Competency probe: Apply If-Match, If-None-Match, and related preconditions to prevent lost updates and duplicate effects. Demonstrate the result through TypeScript 7 strict types plus WHATWG Fetch Request, Response, Headers, URL, streams, and AbortSignal contracts.

For Fetch and TypeScript Test an acceptance boundary · Workshop Preconditions Concurrency, challenge the documented misconception with request, response, fault, policy, and transfer evidence: A client-side timestamp check prevents concurrent write conflicts. This remains false for TypeScript 7 standards-based Fetch.

stimulus.title: Preconditions Concurrency failure evidence
stimulus.caption: A plausible visible result conflicts with the governing contract and requires a cause-level repair.

lines.label: observed
lines.text: A client-side timestamp check prevents concurrent write conflicts. This remains false for TypeScript 7 standards-based Fetch.

lines.label: required
lines.text: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Apply If-Match, If-None-Match, and related preconditions to prevent lost updates and duplicate effects. Demonstrate the result through TypeScript 7 strict types plus WHATWG Fetch Request, Response, Headers, URL, streams, and AbortSignal contracts.

options.text: Reject the unsupported conclusion, name the violated Preconditions Concurrency condition, repair the responsible layer, and rerun the boundary trace.
Keep the plausible output and hide the assumption to avoid confusing the stakeholder. That claim does not survive the test an acceptance boundary evidence boundary.

For test an acceptance boundary, run one convenient Preconditions Concurrency happy path, omit method, authority, credential, byte, time, redirect, retry, and cleanup boundaries, then accept plausible output.

steps.title: Fetch and TypeScript Defend the transferred model · Workshop Preconditions Concurrency
steps.instruction:
Write the workshop evidence note for Preconditions Concurrency: explain the governing condition, one failure, and how this concrete case was verified — accessible staging dashboard SDK must repeat request T-2 after an uncertain timeout: GET status, POST job creation, PUT replacement, and PATCH transition are candidates. Classify safety, idempotency, body replay, and If-Match evidence before choosing. Competency probe: Apply If-Match, If-None-Match, and related preconditions to prevent lost updates and duplicate effects. Demonstrate the result through TypeScript 7 strict types plus WHATWG Fetch Request, Response, Headers, URL, streams, and AbortSignal contracts.

Evidence target: Fetch and TypeScript Defend the transferred model · Workshop Preconditions Concurrency.
steps.why: Retrieval and explanation expose gaps that recognition questions can hide. For Fetch and TypeScript Defend the transferred model · Workshop Preconditions Concurrency, keep Preconditions Concurrency connected to executable HTTP client decision evidence.

content.title: Evidence defense
content.text: Use the case, one assumption, one observable result, and one limitation. Do not copy the competency statement: Apply If-Match, If-None-Match, and related preconditions to prevent lost updates and duplicate effects. Demonstrate the result through TypeScript 7 strict types plus WHATWG Fetch Request, Response, Headers, URL, streams, and AbortSignal contracts.
